title: Module loading baseline

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Module loading baseline; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Module loading baseline
sources.claim: ECMAScript modules have explicit imports and exports, per-module scope, strict semantics, and a dependency graph evaluated once.

canonicalExplanation.summary: ECMAScript modules have explicit imports and exports, per-module scope, strict semantics, and a dependency graph evaluated once.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A named export is imported by exact name and remains linked to the exporting module binding. Your goal is to create and load a minimal two-module browser program. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; html document skeleton; Scripts and console; html navigation urls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: ECMAScript modules have explicit imports and exports, per-module scope, strict semantics, and a dependency graph evaluated once. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Live bindings are not copied values, cycles need careful initialization, and browser imports require resolvable URLs. This draft therefore rejects: Importing a value copies it permanently and each import re-executes the module.

includes: Load a module with deferred execution, strict semantics and explicit imports.
Create and load a minimal two-module browser program.
excludes: Live bindings are not copied values, cycles need careful initialization, and browser imports require resolvable URLs.

outcomes: Create and load a minimal two-module browser program.

examples.title: Module loading baseline: concrete case
examples.situation: A named export is imported by exact name and remains linked to the exporting module binding.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A named export is imported by exact name and remains linked to the exporting module binding.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Module imports are live bindings and a module is evaluated once per realm after dependency linking.
examples.result: The result follows because Module imports are live bindings and a module is evaluated once per realm after dependency linking.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Importing a value copies it permanently and each import re-executes the module.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Module imports are live bindings and a module is evaluated once per realm after dependency linking.
